question: How many total descendants of Jacob entered Egypt according to the OSB tradition?

memoryAid.text: Hold to the number the Church received: seventy-five souls went down into Egypt, as the holy Stephen counted before the council. Where the Hebrew tradition reads seventy, follow the Septuagint and Stephen's tongue: 75 descended, that a multitude might be drawn up. The Apostles themselves read the Greek count we read.
memoryAid.source: Acts 7:14

question: Which of these was NOT named among the sons of Jacob who came into Egypt in this chapter?

memoryAid.text: Joseph is missing from the list of those who 'came into' Egypt because he had gone down before them, into affliction and then into glory, to make ready a place for his kin. Picture the eleven arriving as strangers at the gate while the rejected brother already reigns within it — the one who descends first to gather his brethren after him.

question: A new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.

memoryAid.text: A king arose 'who knew not Joseph' — and in the ancient world to 'know' is to stand in covenant relationship. This Pharaoh does not merely forget; he repudiates the whole bond Joseph made, and severing covenant is what unleashes the death that follows. When a people forgets its deliverer, it is already enslaved before the first whip falls.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Which of the following was NOT one of the store cities the Israelites were forced to build?

memoryAid.text: Store cities: 'POR' — Pithom, On, Raamses. Goshen was where Israel LIVED, given them in Joseph's day; POR is what their backs built in bondage. Do not confuse the place of rest with the work site.

question: Name one of the three store cities the Israelites built for Pharaoh.

memoryAid.text: Pour your labor into 'POR' — Pithom, On (Heliopolis), Raamses. Three storehouse cities that held Pharaoh's grain; Israel's sweat filled the granaries of the very land that enslaved them.

question: Complete the verse: 'The more they afflicted them, the more they ___ and grew.'

memoryAid.text: Picture Egypt pressing down harder and harder, and Israel only swelling larger under the weight — a seed bursts only when buried. The harder the world presses, the wider the promise to Abraham spreads; affliction cannot cancel a divine oath, the press only forces the increase.

question: What were the names of the two Hebrew midwives?

memoryAid.text: 'Shiphrah ships them, Puah pulls them' — say it 'She-frah and Poo-ah.' Two midwives who feared God above the throne; Scripture preserves their names while letting the great king's name fall into silence.

question: The midwives claimed the Hebrew women were too lively to wait for them to arrive in time.

memoryAid.text: 'Lively ladies, late midwives' — the Hebrew women bore before the midwives could arrive. True. Where God means to multiply His people, even the means of death arrive too late; life outruns the executioner.

question: Because the midwives feared God, He provided ___ for them.

memoryAid.text: Because they feared God rather than the king, He 'made them households' — a measure-for-measure reward. They guarded the houses of Israel from being emptied of sons, so God filled their own houses with children. In the biblical economy, to shelter life is repaid with life and lineage.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Into what did Pharaoh command that every newborn Hebrew son be cast?

memoryAid.text: Picture the river Egypt worshiped as a life-giving god turned into a drowning-tomb for infants. By water Pharaoh means to destroy, and by water God will destroy him at the Sea — the very element of his cruelty becomes the instrument of his judgment, while one child floating in a basket overturns the whole decree.

question: How long was the infant Moses hidden by his mother?

memoryAid.text: 'Hidden a trimester' — three months, the length of a pregnancy's first stage. Moses lay concealed as long as a hidden pregnancy can be hidden, until he had to be 'born' a second time onto the waters of the Nile.

question: From what materials was the ark (basket) for baby Moses made?

memoryAid.text: The Greek calls Moses' basket of bulrushes an 'ark' — the same word as Noah's — sealed with asphalt as Noah's was sealed with pitch. Twice God shuts His chosen into a sealed vessel and carries them living through the waters of death; the little ark on the Nile preaches the same salvation the great ark did.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: Why did Pharaoh's daughter go down to the river?

memoryAid.text: Picture the daughter of the king who decreed drowning wading into the very river her father made a grave — and lifting a crying Hebrew son OUT of it alive. The tyrant's own house becomes the rescue and the nursery of Israel's deliverer; God so orders events that the destroyer's daughter undoes him.

question: Who was paid to nurse her own child, Moses?

memoryAid.text: 'Jo-keb-ed got a paycheck' — Jochebed, Moses' own mother, is hired and PAID from Pharaoh's household to nurse her own son. God turns the genocidal court into the wages of redemption, financing the deliverer it means to destroy.

question: Moses went out to his brethren and saw their ___.

memoryAid.text: Reared amid the pleasures of the court, Moses goes out and chooses to see his brethren's burdens rather than enjoy his own ease. This is the soul's first ascent: to lay down comfort and take up the affliction of God's people, esteeming reproach with them greater than the treasures of Egypt — prefiguring the greater Deliverer who, being rich, became poor for our sake.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: Where did Moses hide the Egyptian he had killed?

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses glancing left and right, frantically kicking sand over a body — yet no grave is deep enough to hide a deed from the eyes of God or the tongue of men. By the next day a Hebrew throws it in his face: what is covered in sand surfaces in speech, and the prince must flee.

question: To what land did Moses flee after Pharaoh sought to kill him?

memoryAid.text: Moses runs to MIDian to hide in the MIDdle of the wilderness, and the prince becomes a shepherd. The desert exile is the forge: the man who would lead a people through the wilderness must first be emptied in it for forty years.

question: Moses helped his future wife's family by saving them from shepherds at the well.

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses, fresh off the run, driving off bullying shepherds so seven sisters can water their flock. The shepherd's heart is shown before he is given the flock of Israel — and at a well, as so often in Scripture, a man meets the bride God has prepared. True.

question: By which two names is Moses' father-in-law known?

memoryAid.text: 'JR' — Jethro and Reuel (Raguel), one priest of Midian under two names. Reuel the personal name, Jethro the title. Same father-in-law; do not let the two labels split him into two men.

question: What was the name of Moses' firstborn son?

memoryAid.text: Hear the 'GER' (Hebrew for stranger) in GERshom. Moses brands his firstborn with his own exile: 'I have been a sojourner in a foreign land.' The deliverer names his son for displacement before he ever leads a people home.

question: When Moses killed an Egyptian, whom had the Egyptian been beating?
answer: A Hebrew (one of Moses' own people)
options: A Hebrew (one of Moses' own people)

memoryAid.text: Raised as Pharaoh's grandson, Moses strikes for the slave, not the master. By faith he refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter, choosing to suffer with God's people rather than enjoy Egypt's privilege — his first recorded act already shows which side he is on.
memoryAid.source: Hebrews 11:24-26

question: At the end of chapter 2, God remembered His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

memoryAid.text: God 'remembering' His covenant does not mean He forgot. Picture the cries of Israel rising like smoke and the Lord rising to act: in Scripture, to remember is to move on behalf of a promise. His remembrance here is the trigger of the whole Exodus, deliverance set in motion.

question: By what name is the mountain of God called where Moses saw the burning bush?

memoryAid.text: 'HOReb is where God said HELLO' to Moses — the mountain of God, also called Sinai. One mountain, two names: where the bush now burns, the Law will later thunder on the very same height.

question: The bush burned with fire but was not consumed.

memoryAid.text: The bush that burns yet is not consumed is the great image of the Ever-Virgin, who received the divine Fire in her womb and was not destroyed but made whole. The Light shone forth from the thorns of our nature and burned them not; the radiance of God illumines without devouring. This is the icon of the Unburnt Bush.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses; Orthodox hymnography (the icon of the Unburnt Bush)

question: Moses removed his cloak because he was standing on holy ground.

memoryAid.text: Not cloak — SANDALS. 'Holy ground, shoes off.' Moses removed his sandals, not his cloak; the dead skins bound to the feet must be put off before the living God. The False here is a trap on the wrong garment.

question: What did God command Moses to remove because he stood on holy ground?

memoryAid.text: 'Loose the sandals from your feet, for the place is holy ground.' Gregory teaches that the sandals, the lifeless skins upon the feet, are the dead coverings of earthly attachment; the soul ascending to God must strip away whatever is mortal that clings to it. Bare and unencumbered, only so may one tread where God is.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: What was Moses' first response when God called to him from the bush?

memoryAid.text: 'Here I am' (Hebrew hineni) is the called one's whole-self answer — the very word Abraham, Samuel, and Isaiah each give when God speaks their name. Moses says it before he hears a single command: not 'what do You want?' but 'I am present, I am Yours.'
memoryAid.source: Genesis 22:1; 1 Samuel 3:4; Isaiah 6:8

question: God identified Himself to Moses as the God of which three patriarchs?

memoryAid.text: 'God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' — A-I-J in order, the covenant title that binds the whole story into one act. And because He calls Himself their God still, our Lord taught that these patriarchs live; for He is not God of the dead but of the living, and the three names are a pledge of resurrection.
memoryAid.source: Matthew 22:32

question: The Promised Land is described as a land flowing with milk and ___.

memoryAid.text: Picture rivers of milk and golden honey running down the very hills — abundance you can wade through, the opposite of the brickyard's dust. The land where whips fell gives way to a land that drips with rest; yet the milk and honey are but a shadow of the true homeland prepared for the people of God.

topic: Land Flowing with Milk and Honey
question: Which of these peoples was NOT listed as dwelling in the Promised Land?

memoryAid.text: The seven dwellers: Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites — 'CHAP GHJ.' No Philistines on the list; they are Aegean sea-peoples who arrive on the coast later, never numbered among these seven.

topic: Land Flowing with Milk and Honey
question: Name one of the seven peoples dwelling in the land flowing with milk and honey.

memoryAid.text: Seven nations to dispossess: Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites — 'CHAP GHJ.' Seven is the number of fullness: the land is fully occupied, and God promises to fully give it. Name any of the seven.

question: Complete Moses' question: 'Who am I that I should go to Pharaoh and bring the children of Israel out of ___?'

memoryAid.text: When Moses protests 'Who am I?', God answers not with Moses' qualifications but with His own presence: 'I will be with you.' The credential is never the messenger's worth but the One who sends him — the servant is nothing; the One who goes with him is everything.
memoryAid.source: Exodus 3:11-12

question: By what name did God reveal Himself to Moses (OSB rendering)?

memoryAid.text: In the OSB/Septuagint God names Himself 'I AM THE EXISTING ONE' — He alone truly IS, the source of all being, while creation exists only by sharing in Him. This is why the Church inscribes 'Ho On,' The Existing One, in the halo of Christ on every icon: the same I AM who spoke from the bush is He who said 'Before Abraham was, I AM.'
memoryAid.source: John 8:58

question: How does the OSB render the name God told Moses to give the children of Israel?
answer: The Existing One sent me to you
options: The Existing One sent me to you
The Lord of Hosts sent me

memoryAid.text: 'The Existing One sent me to you' is the messenger-form of the Name of Him who simply IS, who depends on nothing and from whom all dependence flows. This self-existent God of the bush is the very One who later stands in the flesh and declares, 'Before Abraham was, I AM' — the divine Name Christ claims as His own.
memoryAid.source: John 8:58; St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: How many times did Moses object or argue with the Lord before the Lord's anger was kindled?

memoryAid.text: 'Five fingers, five excuses' — count Moses' objections off on one hand, and the fifth ('send another') is what kindled the Lord's anger. God bears with much hesitation, but the refusal of the call itself, not the fear behind it, provokes Him.

question: Which of these was NOT one of the three signs God gave Moses to perform?
options: Hand that becomes leprous and is healed

memoryAid.text: Three signs: Rod-to-serpent, Hand-to-leprous, Water-to-blood. The blossoming almond staff is AARON's later sign (Numbers 17), when God chose the priesthood by a branch that budded — do not smuggle it back into Moses' three.

question: In the first sign, Moses' rod became a ___.

memoryAid.text: Moses' shepherd's staff drops and hisses to life as a serpent, and he recoils — a direct challenge to Pharaoh, whose crown bore the rearing cobra, the uraeus that guarded Egypt's throne. God seizes the very emblem of Pharaoh's power and makes it writhe in a shepherd's hand; taking it up by the tail, Moses grasps the enemy he is sent to overcome.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: In the second sign, Moses' hand became ___ when he drew it from his bosom.

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses drawing his hand from his bosom dead-white with leprosy, then tucking it back and drawing it out whole. In one gesture God shows He both wounds and heals the flesh — the same power that can strike Egypt can as swiftly cleanse, judgment and healing flowing from one bosom.

question: In the third sign, water from the river poured on dry land would become ___.

memoryAid.text: The third sign turns river-water poured on dry ground into blood — the dress rehearsal for plague one, and the target is deliberate. The Nile was worshiped as a god, so the first blow of judgment falls on Egypt's river-deity; the sign in Moses' hand is the seed of the judgment that will redden Egypt's worshiped river.
memoryAid.source: Exodus 12:12

question: Complete Moses' complaint: 'I am weak in speech and slow of ___.'

memoryAid.text: 'Slow of tongue, but sent.' Moses pleads a stammering mouth, yet God chooses the weak speaker to confront the mightiest king on earth — then supplies Aaron's lips, so when Pharaoh falls no one can credit Moses' eloquence. The weakness guarantees the glory stays God's.

question: Whom did God appoint to be Moses' spokesman before Pharaoh?

memoryAid.text: 'Aaron the mouth, Moses the message' — A for Aaron, A for Articulate. Note the order: the word originates with Moses who hears God, and Aaron speaks it — the prophet-and-spokesman pattern, the divine word handed down and proclaimed.

question: Complete God's words to Pharaoh: 'Israel is My ___ son.'

memoryAid.text: 'Israel is My firstborn son' — God claims the nation as His own child and heir among the peoples, and herein lies the legal ground of the last plague: Egypt seized and tried to murder God's firstborn, so Egypt's firstborn must answer. The whole Passover turns on this status of firstborn and inheritance, the shadow of the true Son called out of Egypt.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Complete the warning to Pharaoh: 'If you refuse to let him go, indeed, I will kill your ___ son.'

memoryAid.text: Already in chapter 4 the final plague is announced: free My firstborn, or your firstborn dies. The threat hangs over all ten plagues and is answered only at Passover, where the blood of the lamb marks the doors and spares the sons within — the lamb of whom no bone was broken, whom Melito proclaims as Christ our Pascha slain for us.
memoryAid.source: John 19:36; Melito of Sardis, On Pascha

question: Who circumcised her son after the Lord confronted Moses on the journey?

memoryAid.text: Picture Zipporah's swift flint saving Moses' life. The man sent to demand Pharaoh obey God cannot himself neglect the covenant's sign in his own son; before he can lead the circumcised people to freedom, the seal of the promise must be cut into his own household. Judgment begins at the house of the deliverer.

question: On the journey back to Egypt, the Lord met Moses and sought to kill him until Zipporah circumcised their son.

memoryAid.text: Picture the terrifying night camp: the Lord moves to kill Moses, and Zipporah seizes a flint, circumcises their boy on the spot, and touches Moses' feet with the blood — blood applied to spare a life, a foreshadow of the Passover doorpost just chapters away. The covenant sign is not optional, even for the deliverer. True.

question: Moses and Aaron told Pharaoh: 'Let My people go that they may hold a ______ in the wilderness.'

memoryAid.text: God does not say merely 'let them go' but 'let them go that they may hold a feast unto Me.' Freedom from Egypt is for the sake of worship; liberty's whole end is the festal assembly gathered around the Lord. And the true Feast is Christ our Pascha, sacrificed for us, that we may keep the feast not with old leaven but with sincerity and truth.

question: Pharaoh responded, 'Who is the ______, that I should obey His voice?'

memoryAid.text: Pharaoh's 'Who is the Lord?' is not ignorance but a throne-claim: as a god-king he recognizes no name above his own. The whole plague cycle is the LORD answering that question by judgment against the gods of Egypt (Ex 12:12), so that by the end Egypt will know the very Name it pretended not to know.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: How did Pharaoh respond to the demand to let Israel go?
options: He demanded a tribute of gold
He agreed and then immediately repented

memoryAid.text: Watch the law of the ascent: the moment God says 'let them go,' the tyrant does not loosen his grip but tightens it, piling MORE bricks onto already-bent backs. The squeeze grows heaviest precisely because liberation is now on the table. The pressure after the first 'let go' is not God's abandonment but the enemy's rage at losing his slave.

question: Pharaoh ordered that the Israelites gather their own straw while keeping the same brick quota.

memoryAid.text: Hold the three S's: Same bricks, Stripped straw, Striped backs. Pharaoh keeps the quota high but strips away the means — and the cruelty is engineered to make Israel blame Moses instead of Pharaoh.

question: What building material were the Israelites suddenly required to gather for themselves while still meeting the same brick quota?

memoryAid.text: Straw is the binding that holds the brick together; pull it and the work crumbles, yet the count stays full. 'Snatch the straw, keep the law' — the means removed, the quota unchanged.

question: Who were beaten when the brick quotas were not met?
answer: The Israelite foremen (Clerks in the OSB)
options: The Israelite foremen (Clerks in the OSB)

memoryAid.text: See the Israelite foremen crushed in the middle, taking the lash from Egyptians for a quota Egypt itself made impossible. In their bruised backs, bearing stripes for another's hardness, glimpse faintly the One who would later bear the stripes that were not His own.

question: In Chapter 5, conditions for Israel grew worse before deliverance began.

memoryAid.text: Picture the night at its blackest right before dawn breaks. Chapter 5 ends in the dark on purpose: in the soul's exodus the affliction grows heaviest at the very threshold of deliverance. The deepening groan is not God's absence but the eve of His outstretched arm.

question: God reaffirms His covenant identity to Moses with the declaration, 'I am the ______.'

memoryAid.text: 'I am the LORD' is the divine Name (I AM THE EXISTING ONE) that anchors every promise that follows — the same self-naming spoken from the Bush. When Christ says 'Before Abraham was, I AM,' He claims this very Name. The God who simply IS cannot fail to keep covenant, for He stakes deliverance on His own eternal Being.
memoryAid.source: John 8:58

question: How many major promises did God make to Israel in Exodus 6?

memoryAid.text: Count the 'I wills' and you get SEVEN — the number of the finished work, of rest attained. God does not promise deliverance by halves; the covenant is whole, lacking nothing, as the seven days of creation lacked nothing.

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the seven promises God made in Exodus 6?
answer: I will destroy all of Egypt's gods
options: I will destroy all of Egypt's gods
I will give it to you as an inheritance

memoryAid.text: Sort by the question: is this a gift TO me, or a blow to my captor? The seven 'I wills' are all gifts FOR Israel — bring OUT, rescue, redeem, take, be your God, bring IN, give inheritance. Destroying Egypt's gods is judgment done TO Egypt, real but never among the seven.

question: In the seven promises, God says 'I will ______ you with an outstretched arm' — the act tied to the price of deliverance.

memoryAid.text: 'Redeem' (ga'al) is kinsman-language — to buy back a relative out of slavery, the duty of the near-kinsman. God calls Himself Israel's Redeemer, their next-of-kin, which is exactly what the Word becomes in the flesh: our kinsman who ransoms us with His own blood. This Exodus word runs straight to Pascha.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Among the seven promises, God said 'I will take you as My people, and I will be your God.'

memoryAid.text: 'I will take you as My people, and I will be your God' is the marriage-formula of the covenant, the heart of all the promises. It opens here at the Exodus and closes at the wedding of the Lamb, where God dwells with His people. The whole Exodus aims not at a land but at communion; everything else is for the sake of this one vow.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Why did the Israelites not listen to Moses when he brought God's promises?
answer: Because of discouragement and cruel bondage
options: Because of discouragement and cruel bondage
Because they did not believe in God
Because Moses spoke in a foreign tongue

memoryAid.text: They did not refuse the good news; they could not receive it. The text calls it 'shortness of spirit' — the breath knocked out of them by hard labor. Picture a worker so winded by the whip that hope itself cannot lodge in his lungs. Cruel bondage, not unbelief, stopped their ears.

question: The genealogy in Exodus 6 traces the lineage of which three sons of Israel?

memoryAid.text: The genealogy marches Reuben, Simeon, Levi — then stops dead at Levi. It is no full family tree but a zoom lens, racing past the first two sons to plant the camera on Levi, because the whole point is to certify the priestly bloodline of Moses and Aaron. Count to three and rest at Levi.

question: Which of the following was NOT one of the three sons of Levi?

memoryAid.text: Levi's three sons march in order Gershon, Kohath, Merari — hold 'G-K-M,' the three Levitical clans who carry the Tabernacle. Amram belongs to the next rung of the ladder, son of Kohath, not of Levi; he is a grandson where the question asks for sons.

question: Which son of Levi was the father of Amram (and thus ancestor of Moses and Aaron)?

memoryAid.text: Kohath is the hinge of the holy line — Levi, then Kohath, then Amram, then Aaron and Moses. His clan will one day bear the Ark itself on their shoulders. Remember 'Kohath the conduit,' the channel through which prophet and priest will come.

question: Who was the father of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam?

memoryAid.text: One father, three deliverers: Amram begets Aaron the priest, Moses the prophet, and Miriam the prophetess who leads the women in song. 'Amram's army of three' — one household supplying Israel's whole command.

question: Who was the wife of Amram and mother of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam?

memoryAid.text: Hear the divine Name hiding in hers: JO-chebed carries the theophoric 'Yo-/Yah' — Jochebed means 'the LORD is glory.' She is the one mother whose very name bears God's Name, fitting for the woman who bore and hid the deliverer. Jochebed = God's-Name-mother.

question: Which of the following was NOT a child of Amram and Jochebed?

memoryAid.text: The three of one womb are Aaron, Moses, Miriam (A-M-M). Elisheba enters the house by marriage as Aaron's WIFE, not Jochebed's daughter; watch for the test that swaps a daughter-in-law for a sister.

question: Who was the wife of Aaron?

memoryAid.text: Elisheba IS Elizabeth. The wife of the first high priest bears the name that will one day belong to the mother of the Forerunner (herself of Aaron's daughters, Lk 1:5) — a name woven twice into the priesthood, binding the old covenant priestly line to the herald of the new.
memoryAid.source: Luke 1:5

question: Which of the following was NOT a son of Aaron?

memoryAid.text: Aaron's four sons are Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. Amram cannot be among them, for he stands a generation above as Aaron's father; the line runs Amram down to Aaron, never up. He is root, not branch.

question: How many sons did Aaron have, as listed in Exodus 6?

memoryAid.text: Four sons stand at the altar: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. A tragedy in two halves — two will fall consumed for offering strange fire, and the line survives through the other two. Four given, two faithful, the priesthood preserved by mercy.

question: Exodus 6:23 names Aaron's sons. Besides Nadab and Abihu, name the other two.

memoryAid.text: Line up Aaron's sons in order — Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. The two eldest, Nadab and Abihu, will fall, consumed for offering strange fire before the Lord; the priesthood lives on through Eleazar and Ithamar. A hook for the surviving pair: both names open with a vowel, E and I.

question: God said, 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart and multiply My signs and My ______ in the land of Egypt.'

memoryAid.text: The hardening cuts both ways: the text says Pharaoh hardens his own heart, and God hardens it — the same divine pressure that softens a yielding heart only bakes a defiant one harder, as the selfsame sun melts wax but bakes clay to stone. The 'wonders' multiply because Pharaoh keeps choosing the furnace, making his resistance a theater of God's glory before all nations.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Aaron was 83 years old when he and Moses stood before Pharaoh.

memoryAid.text: 'Aaron Ate 3 more' — A-8-3. The elder brother is three years past 80. The mouth that speaks for God is the younger; the hand that lifts the rod is older.

question: How old was Moses when he stood before Pharaoh?

memoryAid.text: Moses' life is three forties: 40 a prince in Egypt, 40 a shepherd in Midian, 40 a shepherd of Israel. He stands before Pharaoh at 80 — two ages spent, the last and greatest just beginning.

question: When Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh, what did it become?

memoryAid.text: The rod becomes a serpent — and the serpent, the rearing cobra, is the very emblem upon Pharaoh's crown. God lets the symbol of Egyptian sovereignty and of the ancient deceiver rise up only to be swallowed whole, showing the powers of Egypt and the serpent of old already subject to Him.

question: The Egyptian magicians were able to replicate the sign of the rod becoming a serpent.

memoryAid.text: Picture the court sorcerers smugly tossing down their rods, each writhing into a snake by their secret arts — real powers, not sleight of hand — only to watch Aaron's serpent gulp them all down. They could mimic the marvel but never master it.

question: What happened after the magicians' rods also became serpents?
answer: Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods
options: Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods
All the serpents turned back into rods

memoryAid.text: 'Aaron's snake ate the fake snakes.' The first confrontation sets the whole pattern: Egypt's powers produce a counterfeit, but God's true sign devours it whole — a preview of every plague duel to come.

question: What was the first plague God brought upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: Egypt called the Nile a god (Hapi) and drank life from its flood; God turns that very river to blood, judging the idol at the fountain of its life. The opening blow lands squarely on Egypt's chief deity (Ex 12:12), and the 'god' of Egypt bleeds — each plague toppling a trusted god in turn.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Which of the following was NOT among the waters struck in the first plague?

memoryAid.text: Struck: Nile, Streams, Canals, Ponds, Reservoirs — every fresh water Egypt drank, even what was already drawn into vessels. The sea is NOT in the list; the salt water no one drinks is spared, kept for a later and greater drowning.

question: Which of the following was NOT a result of the first plague?
answer: The land was covered in darkness
options: The land was covered in darkness
The Egyptians could not drink the water

memoryAid.text: Results of plague one: fish die, river stinks, no man can drink — all flowing from the bloodied water. Darkness is a different blow entirely, the NINTH plague, far down the road; keep blood by the river and darkness over the land apart.

question: How many days did the first plague (water turned to blood) last?

memoryAid.text: Seven full days the Nile ran red — a complete week-long humiliation of Egypt's river-god before the next blow even begins. 'Seven days of dread by the river of red.'

question: The Egyptian magicians were able to imitate the first plague.

memoryAid.text: Picture the magicians turning a little dish of water red to save face — and so deepening Egypt's misery, not relieving it. The counterfeit can multiply the curse but cannot make one drop clean again. They add to the plague and call it power.

question: What was the second plague God sent upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: The frog was sacred to Heqet, the frog-headed goddess of birth and fertility. God buries Egypt under her own emblem (Ex 12:12) — the symbol of teeming life becomes a teeming horror, so the fertility-goddess is shown powerless to control the very creature that represents her. Every idol is unmasked as powerless.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Who stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt to bring up the frogs?

memoryAid.text: In the early plagues Moses speaks and Aaron acts: it is Aaron's hand and rod stretched over the waters that summon the frogs. 'Aaron's arm brings the swarm' — the prophet's word, the priest's outstretched hand.

question: Which of the following places did the frogs NOT invade?

memoryAid.text: Scripture names where the frogs went: houses, bedchamber, bed, ovens, and kneading bowls (Ex 7:28–8:3) — bed and bread alike defiled. The temples of the gods are never named in the text, so they are the one place not on the list.

question: The frogs entered the Egyptians' ovens and kneading bowls.

memoryAid.text: Reach into the bread dough and draw out a cold, slick frog; lie down and find your bed alive. The plague seizes the most intimate places of life, the oven and the bedchamber, leaving no private corner unbesieged.

question: The magicians were able to bring frogs upon Egypt.

memoryAid.text: The magicians could ADD frogs but never SUBTRACT a single one — a one-way power. They prove they can imitate the plague and thereby only deepen the torment; the taking-away, salvation itself, belongs to God alone.

question: After Pharaoh asked for relief from the frogs, what happened?
answer: Moses prayed and the frogs died
options: Moses prayed and the frogs died
Aaron drove them into the sea
The frogs returned to the Nile alive

memoryAid.text: Watch the tell: Pharaoh begs Moses to pray, because even Pharaoh knows the magicians who conjured frogs cannot banish them. Moses prays, and the frogs die. The enemy can begin a torment, but deliverance comes by prayer alone, from God's hand only.

question: After the frogs died, how were the dead frogs gathered?

memoryAid.text: Picture the dead frogs gathered into reeking heaps across the whole land, the country stinking of yesterday's plague. Even the relief leaves a stench: Pharaoh's delay turns deliverance into a mound of rot — the cost of a hardened heart lingers in the air.

question: Pharaoh kept his promise to let Israel go after the frogs were removed.

memoryAid.text: The instant the frogs die, Pharaoh forgets his vow — repentance that lasts only as long as the pain. Here is the very portrait of false turning: fear cries out under the lash and rebels the moment comfort returns. Beware repentance that breathes only in affliction and never reaches the will.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: What was the third plague God sent upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: Run the order B-F-G: Blood, Frogs, Gnats. The third plague — gnats/lice — is the first the magicians cannot copy, the line where they cry 'the finger of God.'

question: What did Aaron strike to bring forth the third plague?

memoryAid.text: From the dust man was first formed, and from the dust the gnats now swarm — to bring life from dust is the Creator's own signature. That is exactly why the magicians fail here and confess 'the finger of God': they can mimic transformation but cannot create life from earth. The dust obeys only Him who first shaped it.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: The magicians were able to duplicate the third plague of lice/gnats.

memoryAid.text: At the third plague the imitators fail at last. 'Three and done' — they could counterfeit blood and frogs, but the gnats stop them cold, and even they must name the finger of God.

question: Unable to duplicate the third plague, the magicians said to Pharaoh, 'This is the ______ of God.'

memoryAid.text: 'This is the finger of God' is the magicians' surrender, spoken by the enemy's own priests when their arts collapse at the gnats. That same finger will write the Law on Sinai's tablets, and Christ will cast out demons 'by the finger of God' (Lk 11:20) — where human counterfeit ends, the immediate touch of God begins.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: What was the fourth plague God sent upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: After blood, frogs, and gnats comes the fourth: swarms of dog-flies filling the air. 'Four for the flies that fill the skies' — the fourth blow that darkens Egypt's sky with wings.

question: During the fourth plague, which region was spared from the dog-flies (swarms of flies)?

memoryAid.text: 'Goshen = Go safe.' The dwelling of the people of God is the one place the dog-flies cannot enter; while all Egypt is blackened with swarms, Israel's land is sealed off untouched.

question: The fourth plague marks the first time God makes a clear separation between Israel and Egypt, protecting His people while the Egyptians suffered.

memoryAid.text: Picture a clear line drawn across the land: dog-flies swarm everywhere in Egypt, but Goshen, where Israel dwells, stays untouched. Beginning with the flies, God 'sets apart' His own — the plagues are no blind calamity but the targeted acts of the Shepherd who knows His sheep by name, a foretaste of the Passover separation to come.

question: After the fourth plague, Pharaoh immediately allowed Israel to leave Egypt without conditions.

memoryAid.text: Watch Pharaoh negotiate: worship inside Egypt, or 'do not go far.' Every offer keeps a leash on Israel, a half-deliverance keeping them within reach of bondage — and then he hardens his heart again. The Exodus rejects the half-measure: true going-out is complete, never a managed truce with Egypt.

question: Which of the following is the correct order of the second, third, and fourth plagues?

memoryAid.text: After blood (1): Frogs, Gnats, Flies — '1-2-3-4: Blood, Frogs, Gnats, Flies.' The hinge to remember is the gnats: the magicians keep pace through the frogs, then break at the gnats, so the order also marks where Egypt's counterfeit power dies.

question: The fifth plague was a disease that struck Egypt's ______.

memoryAid.text: Egypt deified its herds, the Apis bull and the cow-goddess Hathor. When the Lord strikes the livestock He is not killing random cattle; He is executing judgment on the gods of Egypt, the very beasts they bowed to. Plague five is a divine raid on Egypt's barn full of idols.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

question: Which of the following animals was NOT named among those struck by the fifth plague?

memoryAid.text: The named five in order: Horses, Donkeys, Camels, Oxen, Sheep, 'Happy Donkeys Carry Old Sheep.' The goat alone is absent from the list; let its absence be your answer.

question: Israel's livestock died along with the livestock of Egypt.

memoryAid.text: Notice the verb Exodus uses: the Lord 'separates' Israel's cattle from Egypt's, the same separating He did at creation between light and dark. Not one beast of Israel died though every Egyptian herd was struck. Goshen becomes a pocket of the new creation inside the old, the line of the elect drawn already before Passover marks it in blood.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God

question: What did Moses toss into the air to bring about the sixth plague?

memoryAid.text: Watch Moses fling a double handful of furnace ashes toward heaven, the soot of the very kiln where Hebrew slaves baked Pharaoh's bricks. The gray dust drifts down over all the land and blooms into festering boils on man and beast. The instrument of Israel's slavery rains back as judgment on the slavemasters.

question: In the sixth plague, Moses' furnace ashes caused ______ that broke out in sores on both man and beast.

memoryAid.text: Ashes up, boils down: the furnace soot Moses flings skyward settles as boils that break open into sores (LXX 'sore blains') — and they fall on man AND beast alike. Boils swell; sores break.

question: During the sixth plague the magicians could not stand before Moses, because the sores had broken out on them as well.

memoryAid.text: Verse 11 turns the knife: the sorcerers who matched Moses' early signs cannot even stand before him now, for the sores were on the magicians themselves. Egypt's supposed mediators of the gods are exposed as powerless flesh, erupting with the same boils as every slave and beast.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: What unusual thing flashed within the hail of the seventh plague?

memoryAid.text: See ice and fire falling as one: flaming hail streaking down while fire runs along the ground. A sky divided against itself, ice and flame agreeing only to destroy.

question: Which two crops were destroyed by the hail?

memoryAid.text: 'Flax and Barley Bit the dust' -- the early B-crop and the flax were ripe and exposed, so the hail shattered them. Wheat and spelt came later and lived; ripeness is what got them killed.

question: Which two crops survived the hail because they ripen later?

memoryAid.text: 'Wheat and Spelt came Late, so they Wait.' Not yet risen above the ground, the latecomers were hidden from the hail; what had not grown up could not be cut down.

question: What did Moses do to bring the seventh plague of hail upon Egypt?
answer: Stretched out his rod/hand toward heaven
options: Stretched out his rod/hand toward heaven
Struck the dust of the ground
Stretched his hand over the river

memoryAid.text: Match the gesture to the plague: ashes-up = boils, strike-the-DUST = gnats, hand-over-the-RIVER = blood, rod-to-the-SKY = hail. For hail the blow falls from above, so Moses reaches toward heaven.

question: Everyone in Egypt lost their servants and animals to the hail.

memoryAid.text: This is the first plague where Scripture records Egyptians choosing sides: whoever feared the word of the Lord drove his servants and cattle under shelter and was spared. Even among Pharaoh's officials the fear of the Lord creates a remnant. The line of salvation is drawn by faith, not by ethnicity, well before Passover.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: During the hail, Pharaoh confessed, 'I have ______ this time.'

memoryAid.text: Hear Pharaoh cry 'I have sinned!' over the roar of the thunder, then watch his face harden the instant the hail stops. A confession molded out of ice, melting the moment the danger passes.

question: Pharaoh truly repented after confessing his sin during the hail.

memoryAid.text: Pharaoh confessed with his mouth while his heart stayed stone, and the moment the hail ceased he sinned again. This is the sorrow of the world that fears the lash but not the Lord; godly grief changes the man, but worldly regret only wants the storm to stop.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: The eighth plague brought a swarm of ______ upon Egypt.

memoryAid.text: Eight = ATE. The eighth plague is the plague that ate, the locusts devouring everything the hail had spared. Say 'eight ate' and you cannot forget it.

question: Which wind did God use to bring the locusts upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: The south wind is the Lord's wind that brought the locusts upon Egypt.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

question: The locusts ate everything that was left after the hail.

memoryAid.text: See the very wheat and spelt that survived the hail now stripped to bare stalks. A living green tide of insects pours over the land and swallows the last harvest Egypt had left; nothing green remained.

question: Pharaoh's servants asked, 'How long shall this man be a ______ to us?'

memoryAid.text: See Pharaoh's own court turn and point at Moses: 'How long shall this man be a snare to us?' His own servants see the trap closing before the king will admit it.

question: During the locust plague, Pharaoh offered to let everyone in Israel leave.

memoryAid.text: Pharaoh would loose only the men and hold the little ones back as a pledge of return. This is ever the enemy's bargain, to grant a partial freedom that he may keep his grip; a worship that leaves your family in Egypt is exactly the deal the deceiver wants you to take.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: The ninth plague covered Egypt in thick ______.

memoryAid.text: Nine = Night. The ninth plague turned Egypt into one unbroken night for three days; let the rhyme of nine and night hold the number fast.

question: How long did the darkness cover the land of Egypt?

memoryAid.text: Three days of darkness, and the number rhymes forward: three days in the belly of the fish, three days in the tomb. Egypt is buried in a darkness it can feel for the very span of death before resurrection. Darkness for THREE.

question: The Israelites also sat in darkness like the Egyptians.

memoryAid.text: Egypt sat in darkness that could be felt, yet all Israel had light in their dwellings. This is the original division of the children of light from the children of darkness; the selfsame Presence that is blinding gloom to Egypt is warmth and light to those who abide with Him.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: After the darkness, Pharaoh offered to let everyone go except their livestock.

memoryAid.text: Watch Pharaoh haggle like a man who refuses to release his hostages: first 'only the men may go,' now 'everyone — but the flocks stay behind.' Every concession still clutches something back; this time the livestock are the chain he hopes will drag Israel home.

question: Moses replied that not one ______ of their livestock would be left behind.

memoryAid.text: See Moses plant his staff: 'Not one hoof stays behind!' Every beast is needed for sacrifice to the Lord; God's people depart whole, leaving nothing of themselves in Egypt's hand.

question: After the darkness, one final plague still remained for Egypt.

memoryAid.text: Nine plagues have been escalating skirmishes; the tenth is the killing blow aimed at the heir. In the ancient world the firstborn son is the future, the inheritance, the dynasty, so the final plague does not just wound Egypt, it ends Egypt's tomorrow. One blow left, and it lands on the throne's successor.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

question: God declared that about ______ He would go out through the midst of Egypt.

memoryAid.text: Fix the hour at midnight, the deepest point of the night, when the Lord Himself goes out through the midst of Egypt. He walks straight into death's own hour and turns it into the hour of deliverance, the same midnight at which the Bridegroom comes and the dead will rise.

question: Israel was told to leave Egypt immediately, before the final plague struck.

memoryAid.text: Israel did not bolt in panic; they waited, and what they waited for was a liturgy. Before the Exodus there must be the Passover meal, given in the very next chapter; deliverance comes through worship, not flight. God feeds His people on the Lamb at the altar before He leads them out the door.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: Israel was promised they would leave Egypt carrying what?

memoryAid.text: This is not looting but back-wages, and deeper, the spoils of a defeated army. The Divine Warrior has conquered Egypt and His people carry off the plunder, silver and gold that will become the gold of the tabernacle. Egypt's treasure is conscripted into the worship of the God who beat its gods.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

question: What was required of the Passover lamb's condition?

memoryAid.text: A lamb without blemish, because only the flawless can stand in the place of the firstborn. Melito saw it plainly: the spotless lamb is the type of the spotless Christ, the only one who can offer Himself for the life of all. The blemish test is a christology lesson.
memoryAid.source: Melito of Sardis, On Pascha

question: What sex and age was the Passover lamb to be?

memoryAid.text: A MALE in his first YEAR, without blemish: 'a young male, in his prime, no flaw.' Three marks to keep: male, year-old, perfect.

question: The Passover lamb was to be a female of the flock.

memoryAid.text: Not female but MALE, because the lamb dies in the place of the firstborn SON of the house. The substitution only works son-for-son; that is why John the Baptist points and says 'Behold the Lamb,' not a ewe. Male, year-old, unblemished.
memoryAid.source: St. John the Forerunner (John 1:29)

question: On which day of the month was the Passover lamb to be set apart?

memoryAid.text: '10 to inspect, 14 to perfect.' Set apart on the 10th and slain on the 14th, four days of proving show the lamb is truly without blemish before it dies.

question: On which day of the month was the Passover lamb to be killed?

memoryAid.text: '10 to inspect, 14 to perfect.' Chosen on the 10th, killed on the 14th between dusk and dark, when the days of inspection are done.

question: Where was the blood of the Passover lamb to be applied?

memoryAid.text: Blood up the two side posts and across the top lintel: Justin Martyr already saw the doorway traced in blood as the shape of the Cross. The household is saved by passing under a bloodied cross-frame, centuries before Golgotha. The saving sign points up and out, never trampled below.
memoryAid.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 111

question: The Passover blood was to be placed on the threshold of the door.

memoryAid.text: Side posts and lintel, never the threshold, because you will not tread the saving blood underfoot. The geometry itself is theology: the Cross is over you, never under your shoe; the blood of the Lamb is reverenced and lifted up, not stepped upon.
memoryAid.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 111

question: Which three things made up the Passover meal?
answer: Lamb roasted with fire, unleavened bread, and bitter herbs
options: Lamb roasted with fire, unleavened bread, and bitter herbs
Boiled lamb, leavened bread, and sweet herbs
Roasted lamb, unleavened bread, and honey
Raw lamb, unleavened bread, and bitter herbs

memoryAid.text: Three for the meal, 'Fire, Flat, and Bitter': lamb roasted by Fire, Flat unleavened bread, and Bitter herbs. The fire of the Passion, the sinless bread, the bitterness of bondage, all eaten in one night.

question: How was the Passover lamb to be cooked?

memoryAid.text: Roasted whole over fire, never boiled, never raw. Justin records that the lamb was spitted on two crossed skewers, head-to-tail and across, so the very lamb on the fire hung in the shape of a cross. The cooking method is a crucifix, the Lamb wholly given over in His Passion.
memoryAid.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 111

question: The Passover bread was to be leavened.

memoryAid.text: Unleavened, because leaven is the biblical image of corruption spreading through the lump, and there was no time to rise anyway. Paul makes it the Christian's command: keep the feast not with the old leaven of malice but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth, for Christ our Passover is sacrificed for us.
memoryAid.source: 1 Corinthians 5:7-8

question: Along with the lamb and unleavened bread, the meal included bitter ______.

memoryAid.text: The bitter herbs are the taste of slavery, eaten beside the unleavened bread so the memory of bondage is never lost. You taste the bondage even as you celebrate the rescue; keep the feast, then, not with the old leaven of malice but with sincerity and truth, recalling the bitterness from which Christ our Passover has set us free.
memoryAid.source: 1 Corinthians 5:7-8

question: Of this feast God declared, 'It is the Lord's ______.'

memoryAid.text: Pascha means the 'passing over' of the destroyer, and the name itself is the gospel in one verb. Melito sees it fulfilled in Christ the slain Lamb, by whose own blood death passes over us and we pass over from death into life.
memoryAid.source: Melito of Sardis, On Pascha

question: Who died in the tenth and final plague?

memoryAid.text: Every firstborn, and Exodus tells you why: 'against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment.' Pharaoh was reckoned a living god whose divine line ran through his firstborn, so striking every firstborn topples the whole pantheon and its incarnate king in one night. From the palace to the prison, no house untouched but those under the blood.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

question: Who was spared from the death of the firstborn?

memoryAid.text: Only the blood on the door saved the house, not flight, not merit, not hiding. So too only the blood of Christ shelters us; the destroyer passes over all who are marked by the Lamb, a salvation given by grace through the sign of sacrifice.
memoryAid.source: Melito of Sardis, On Pascha

question: Pharaoh's own son died in the final plague.

memoryAid.text: Pharaoh's firstborn was the next living god of Egypt, the heir of the sun-god's throne. When he dies, the entire theology of Egyptian kingship dies with him in a single night; the god-king watches his own divine succession struck down. The hardest heart finally pays in the currency it valued most.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

question: How many years did Israel sojourn in Egypt and Canaan?

memoryAid.text: 'Four-three-oh, time to go.' The Septuagint counts 430 years as the full span of sojourning in Egypt and Canaan together; on the very day they ended, the host of the Lord went out, deliverance arriving exactly on time.

question: About how many men left Egypt, not counting women and children?

memoryAid.text: 'Six hundred thousand, and many more besides.' About 600,000 men on foot, and that is before a single woman or child is counted, a whole nation born in a night.

question: The figure of about 600,000 leaving Egypt counted only the men, not the women and children.

memoryAid.text: The 600,000 counts only the fighting-age men, and that word matters, because Exodus calls this host the Lord's armies marching out in battle array. The census is a muster roll; what leaves Egypt is not just a people but an army, with the women and children filling in a whole nation behind the ranks.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

question: How were the Israelites told to eat the first Passover?
answer: In haste, dressed to travel (loins girded, sandals on, staff in hand)
options: In haste, dressed to travel (loins girded, sandals on, staff in hand)
Reclining at ease around the table
After a full day of fasting

memoryAid.text: Picture a family eating standing up — belts cinched, sandals laced, staff in hand, food bolted down — a meal eaten like people about to run for their lives, because they were. The Passover is a traveler's supper, eaten ready to leave Egypt behind.

question: In Exodus 13, every firstborn belongs to God.

memoryAid.text: In the ancient world the firstborn son was the heir who carried the father's name; on Passover night the destroyer passed over Israel's firstborn, so God now claims that place by right of redemption. Every firstborn becomes a living receipt of the ransom: where the lamb's blood once stood, a whole life is owed back. The One God spared, God owns.
memoryAid.source: St. Cyril of Alexandria, Glaphyra on Exodus

question: How many days did the Feast of Unleavened Bread last?

memoryAid.text: Seven days, the span of a creation week — a whole cycle of time must pass before the soul is wholly purged of Egypt's leaven, the old life puffing itself up. As the Apostle says, keep the feast not with the old leaven of malice but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.

question: God led Israel by the shortest route to Canaan.

memoryAid.text: The Philistine coast road was the fast lane to Canaan, but it ran straight through fortified war-country. A nation of just-freed slaves would see armies and bolt back to the flesh-pots; so the Man of War leads them the long way round, training them in the desert before He ever lets them fight.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: By what did the Lord lead Israel during the day?

memoryAid.text: Cloud for cover by DAY, fire for light by NIGHT — shade when the sun blazes, flame when it is dark. The same Presence meets you in whatever the hour lacks: darkness needs no shade, so the pillar burns.

question: By what did the Lord lead Israel during the night?

memoryAid.text: By night the pillar is fire — the same Presence that burned in the Bush and would settle on Sinai, the Glory of the Lord traveling at the head of His host. Remember it by opposites: when the world is dark, the column burns, for the Light shines precisely where there is no other light.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: What did Moses bring with him out of Egypt, fulfilling an ancient promise?

memoryAid.text: On the most chaotic night in Israel's history, picture Moses hauling a centuries-old mummy-coffin out of Egypt. Joseph had made them swear it four hundred years before — 'carry up my bones' — and now the man who once went down in chains leads the march to freedom, riding toward the inheritance he never stopped believing in.

question: Whose promise was fulfilled when Moses carried bones out of Egypt?
answer: Joseph's, that he would reach the promised land
options: Joseph's, that he would reach the promised land
Abraham's, that he would be buried in Canaan
Jacob's, that he would see Egypt again
Levi's, that his tribe would serve the altar

memoryAid.text: Joseph's bones, Joseph's oath — not Abraham, not Jacob, not Levi. The man who saved Egypt from famine bet his very remains on the promise; bind the bones to the name and the name to the promise: Joseph's remains, Joseph's vow, Joseph going home to Canaan.

question: Where did Israel camp before the sea?
answer: Opposite Baal Zephon, between Migdol and the sea
options: Opposite Baal Zephon, between Migdol and the sea
At Elim among the springs and palms
At Marah by the bitter waters

memoryAid.text: Notice the place they're pinned against: Baal Zephon, 'lord of the north,' a shrine to the Canaanite storm-god who supposedly ruled the sea. Migdol behind, the deep ahead, a rival sea-deity's temple looking on — YHWH deliberately corners Israel at the idol's doorstep, then splits that very sea to show whose hand commands the waters.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Religion of the Apostles (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Israel camped opposite Baal Zephon, between the sea and what fortified place?

memoryAid.text: MIGDOL means 'tower' — an Egyptian frontier fortress. Fix the trap by its watchtower: a tower on one side, the deep on the other, a sea-god's shrine ahead. The fortress that pens them in marks the very spot where the sea becomes a road.

question: Israel was confident when Pharaoh approached at the sea.

memoryAid.text: Two million people pinned against the water, dust rising from Pharaoh's chariots behind — and they cry out in terror, not confidence, accusing Moses of dragging them out to die. It is the first murmuring, the same fear that will keep that whole generation out of the land: a freed slave still thinks like a slave.

question: Complete the verse: 'The Lord will fight for you, and you shall hold your ____.'

memoryAid.text: 'Hold your peace' is a soldier's command to a terrified levy: stand still, the Captain fights. This is holy war where Israel does not lift a sword — the Lord is a Man of War, and the only thing the army must do is stop screaming and watch Him divide the waters. Stillness here is not idleness but faith.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: According to the OSB, by what kind of wind was the sea divided?

memoryAid.text: Mind the Septuagint: a strong SOUTH wind drove back the sea, where Hebrew-based Bibles read 'east.' That wind is the Spirit-breath of God brooding over the waters as at creation, and Paul reads the whole crossing as our Baptism — our fathers were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea, delivered through the font from slavery into life.

question: The Egyptians crossed the sea safely behind Israel.

memoryAid.text: Not one Egyptian crossed: the same water that is a wall for Israel is a grave for Egypt. So the font itself is twofold — life to the one passing through, death to the sin and the Pharaoh-devil that pursue. What saves the new man drowns the old.
memoryAid.source: Theodoret of Cyrus, Questions on Exodus

question: What happened to Pharaoh's army at the Red Sea?
answer: The chariots and horsemen perished in the returning waters
options: The chariots and horsemen perished in the returning waters
They turned back to Egypt unharmed
They crossed and pursued Israel into the wilderness
They were struck blind and wandered off

memoryAid.text: Egypt's chariot corps was the tank division of the Bronze Age, the height of imperial war-tech. Picture the wheels miring in the sea-floor mud as the walls of water collapse — horse and rider, chariot and charioteer swept under in an instant, the strongest army on earth unmade without Israel landing a single blow.

question: Complete the opening of the Song of Moses: 'Let us sing to the Lord, for he is very greatly glorified: horse and rider he has thrown into the ____.'

memoryAid.text: 'Horse and rider He has thrown into the SEA' — a war-hymn, the oldest victory song in Scripture, sung over a drowned army. The Church makes it the First Ode of every canon: we sing Moses' battle-song because the Red Sea is our Baptism and Pharaoh is the enemy we just watched drown. The soul brought through the water does not argue; it sings.

question: Complete the verse: 'He was to me a helper and protector for salvation: this is my God, and I will ____ him.'

memoryAid.text: Helper, Protector, Salvation — God's three acts — then the soul's one answer: 'I will GLORIFY Him.' Theology before doxology: He does all the saving, we do the glorifying. Let the cascade carry you to the verb.

question: Complete the verse: 'The Lord bringing wars to nought, the Lord is his ____.'

memoryAid.text: 'The Lord bringing wars to nought, the Lord is His NAME.' What He does (ends wars) is who He is. To know the Name is to know the One who ends battles by winning them — tie the deed to the title and 'name' is the answer.

question: What title is given to Miriam in the Song of Moses?

memoryAid.text: Miriam is called 'prophetess' — the first woman in Scripture to bear the title, taking the timbrel not as queen or priestess but because the Spirit gives her the song. She stands in a line of singing prophetesses, Deborah and Hannah, that runs to the Theotokos, whose Magnificat answers Miriam's timbrel across the Red Sea of history.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Miriam led the women with dance and what instrument?

memoryAid.text: Picture Miriam at the head of a line of women, timbrels ringing and feet dancing on the dry far shore while Egypt floats face-down behind them. The drum and the dance are liturgical, not casual — this is the first thanksgiving service of the redeemed, music born the instant the danger drowned.

question: The people found fresh, sweet water when they arrived at Marah.

memoryAid.text: MARAH is the very word 'bitter' — the place is named for what the tongue tasted. The name is the answer: no sweet water here, only bitterness waiting for the wood.

question: What did Moses cast into the bitter waters of Marah to make them sweet?

memoryAid.text: The wood cast into the bitter water is the Cross cast into the bitterness of our mortal life: what was undrinkable becomes sweet the moment the tree is plunged in. Death's bitterness is not removed but transfigured by the wood, as wood lifted up heals the world.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: Complete the verse: 'I am the Lord thy God that ____ thee.'

memoryAid.text: The Lord who heals reveals Himself at the very spot where He heals — Marah, the bitter, made sweet by wood. Read the title through the type: the Physician's medicine is the Cross plunged into our embittered nature, and only there does He name Himself 'the Lord that heals thee.'
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: How many springs of water did Israel find at the oasis of Elim?

memoryAid.text: Twelve springs for twelve tribes that will become twelve Apostles — fountains enough that each may drink from its own. After the bitter wood of Marah, the soul is led to twelve springs and seventy palms: the Church prefigured as an oasis, water that no longer needs healing.
memoryAid.source: St. Cyril of Alexandria, Glaphyra on Exodus

question: How many palm trees were at the oasis of Elim?

memoryAid.text: Seventy palms over twelve springs — seventy like the disciples Christ appointed and sent ahead two by two, twelve like the Apostles. Fix SEVENTY to the trees that give shade and rest after Marah's bitterness, and the whole apostolic Church appears at the oasis.

question: How many days did Israel travel in the wilderness without finding water, before reaching the bitter springs of Marah?

memoryAid.text: Three waterless days under the desert sun — then at last, water! — but it is bitter. Picture the crushing letdown at Marah: relief curdling into a groan. Three days parched makes the bitter spring sting all the more.

question: On what day did Israel come to the Wilderness of Sin?
answer: The fifteenth day of the second month
options: The fifteenth day of the second month
The first day of the first month
The fortieth day after the Passover
The seventh day of the third month

memoryAid.text: The FIFTEENTH of the SECOND month — one month to the day since they left Egypt on the fifteenth of the first. Count by fifteens: the Passover's provisions run dry exactly one moon-cycle into freedom.

question: Israel reached the Wilderness of Sin about one month after leaving Egypt.

memoryAid.text: Out on the fifteenth, hungry on the fifteenth a month later — one month to the day. The number that frees them is the number that finds them empty; count by fifteens and you cannot miss it.

question: Israel journeyed from Elim into which wilderness, where the manna was given?

memoryAid.text: From Elim's springs to the Wilderness of SIN — beware the trap in the name: this 'Sin' is tied to SINAI, the holy mountain it lies near, not the word for transgression. The manna falls in the desert named for the mountain.

question: What did the people say they had sat beside in Egypt when they complained of hunger?

memoryAid.text: They sigh for the 'pots of meat' — the very cauldrons of their slavery — saying they'd rather have died full as slaves than walk free and hungry. This is Egypt's deepest chain: not the whip but the memory of its meat, the belly that begs to go back. The soul half-freed still craves the savory pots of its old bondage.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: In chapter 16 Israel went from singing God's praises to complaining about hunger.

memoryAid.text: Weeks earlier this crowd sang 'horse and rider He has thrown into the sea'; now the same throats grumble over an empty supper. From the Song of the Sea to murmuring over bread is the whole arc of the human heart — the warning Paul hands the Church not to crave again what God delivered us from.

question: When did the manna appear each day?

memoryAid.text: Bread with the MORNING dew, meat with the EVENING dusk — God runs a two-shift kitchen in the desert. Manna comes with the rising light, quail with the falling: tie bread to dawn.

question: When did the quail appear?

memoryAid.text: Quail at EVENING, manna at morning — flesh by night, bread by day. The dusk brings meat to the camp, so fasten quail to the falling of the sun.

question: What does the word 'manna' mean?

memoryAid.text: 'Manna' is literally the bewildered question 'What is it?' — Israel names the bread after its own confusion, for the heavenly food outruns every category the mind can name. The question is not fully answered until Christ stands in John 6 and says 'I am the bread of life': the manna stays a question until the Word makes Himself the reply.

question: Israel could store leftover manna for the next day without it spoiling.

memoryAid.text: Hoarded manna breeds WORMS and stinks by morning — only the Sabbath portion keeps. You cannot stockpile this bread; gather it fresh each dawn. The whole catechism in a food: 'give us this day our daily bread.'

question: How much manna did Israel gather on the sixth day?
options: The same as every other day

memoryAid.text: Sixth day, DOUBLE portion — gather twice so the seventh can be wholly rest. The Sabbath is baked into the manna before Sinai ever legislates it: rest is something God lays up in advance.

question: According to Exodus 16:8, against whom was Israel's murmuring truly directed?

memoryAid.text: Picture the people shaking their fists at Moses and Aaron — but Moses turns the fists aside: 'your murmurings are not against us but against the Lord.' To despise the gift is to despise the Giver; the complaint sails past the stewards and strikes the King who sent the bread.

question: According to Exodus 16:8, the Lord gives meat in the evening and what in the morning?

memoryAid.text: Meat in the EVENING, bread in the MORNING — evening meat, morning bread. The verse pairs them precisely so Israel knows both came from the Lord, not from luck or the land.

question: How much manna was each person to gather according to Exodus 16:16?

memoryAid.text: One omer for each head — and when they measured it, he who gathered much had nothing over, and he who gathered little had no lack. (Reference Exodus 16:18 for context)

question: According to Exodus 16:31, what was the taste of the manna like?

memoryAid.text: White like coriander seed, sweet as a wafer made with honey — the desert tasting honey from the sky. The sweetness is a hint of what it signifies: Christ the Bread of Life, the true bread come down from heaven, sweeter than any honeycomb to the soul that eats Him.

question: Manna was white and like the seed of what plant in appearance?

memoryAid.text: Picture the desert floor at dawn dusted white like coriander seed, each tiny pale grain glistening with dew — bread from heaven sown like seed on the ground for the gathering. Small, white, round: the very look of manna.

question: For how many years did Israel eat manna?

memoryAid.text: FORTY years of wandering, FORTY years of manna — bread from heaven the whole pilgrimage long, never failing till the Land. Forty is the number of testing, and every single day of it God set a table in the wilderness: forty matched to forty.

question: Israel ate manna until they reached the border of which land?

memoryAid.text: The manna fell forty years and stopped the very day Israel reached the border of Phoenicia (Exod 16:35) — wilderness bread giving way to the land's produce.
memoryAid.source: Joshua 5:12

question: Which of these is NOT something that happened in the Wilderness of Sin?
answer: Water flowed from a struck rock
options: Water flowed from a struck rock
A double portion was gathered on the sixth day

memoryAid.text: Chapter 16 is bread-and-quail country; the water from the struck rock is the NEXT episode, chapter 17 at Massah and Meribah. Sort it by element — here it falls from the sky, there it flows from the stone — and don't merge the two desert miracles, for Paul says the Rock that followed them was Christ.

question: At what place did Israel camp when there was no water, leading to the water from the rock?

memoryAid.text: Rephidim means 'resting-places' — yet there the people found no rest, only dry throats and a quarrel. Picture them slumping down at a campsite named 'Rest' and finding not a drop of water, until God splits open the Rock. The place called Rest gave no rest until the Rock was struck.

question: Moses struck the rock to bring forth water.

memoryAid.text: The Rock yields nothing until it is smitten. Moses strikes it once, and a river gushes for a whole thirsting nation. St. Paul says plainly, 'that Rock was Christ' — the Rock struck so that living water flows is the Crucified, smitten on the Cross, from whose pierced side blood and water pour.
memoryAid.source: 1 Corinthians 10:1-4

question: At which mountain did God tell Moses he would stand on the rock to bring forth water?

memoryAid.text: Horeb and Sinai are two names for one mountain — the mount of God, of the burning bush and the Law. The water flows from the rock at that same mount of revelation: where God appeared in fire and gave the Law, He now gives water from the rock. The mount of revelation is also the mount of provision.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: What two names did Moses give the place where the people tested the Lord?

memoryAid.text: One place, two indictments, two M's: Temptation (Testing — they put God on trial) and Abuse (Contention — they quarreled as if He had failed them). The OSB wording is "Temptation and Abuse."

question: The OSB names the place “Temptation and Abuse” (Ex 17:7). Which two sins of Israel does that name record?
answer: Tempting the Lord and quarreling against Him
options: Tempting the Lord and quarreling against Him
Fear of the Amalekites and unbelief
Pride against Moses and envy of Aaron

memoryAid.text: Forget Massah/Meribah — the OSB calls the place “Temptation and Abuse” (Ex 17:7). Temptation: they put the Lord on trial, “Is the Lord among us or not?” Abuse: their abusive, contentious words against Moses and God. The place-name is the verdict on their hearts.

question: Who led Israel's army into battle against Amalek?

memoryAid.text: Joshua — Iesous in the Greek — bears the very name of the Saviour, 'the Lord saves.' It is he, not Moses, who descends to wage war on Amalek: the Law (Moses) lifts the cross-form on the height while Jesus conquers our enemy in the field. The name on the battle-standard is already the Name above every name.
memoryAid.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 90

question: What happened to the battle whenever Moses raised his hands?

memoryAid.text: The battle is won not by the sword below but by the shape of the arms above: while Moses holds the form of the Cross, Israel prevails; let his hands drop, and Amalek surges. Justin Martyr saw it plainly — victory hangs upon the Cross, not the spear.
memoryAid.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 90

question: Who went up to the top of the hill with Moses during the battle with Amalek?

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses crucified in posture on the hilltop, arms flung wide, with Aaron the priest bracing one arm and Hur the other — priesthood and people flanking him left and right, as the Lord would one day hang between two others on the hill of His passion. Two men, two arms, one unbroken Cross until sunset.

question: How did Aaron and Hur help Moses keep his hands raised?
answer: They put a stone under him and held up his hands

memoryAid.text: They seat Moses on a stone and brace each arm until the sun sets, so the figure of the Cross never drops for an instant all day long. It takes three men to keep the Cross raised — the Body upholding the form by which Joshua (Jesus) conquers. Perseverance in the Cross unto the very end wins the war.
memoryAid.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 90

options: They put a stone under him and held up his hands
They prayed aloud for him from a distance
They fought alongside Joshua in the valley
They carried a banner above his head

question: What name did Moses give the altar he built after defeating Amalek?

memoryAid.text: Moses' arms, held up by Aaron and Hur, were Israel's living battle-standard high on the hill; when the fight was won he planted an altar and named it after the true standard — 'The Lord My Refuge.' Picture the raised arms becoming the flagpole the whole army rallies to: the God who fought through those hands is Himself the banner of His host.

question: Who came to visit Moses in the wilderness, bringing his wife and sons?

memoryAid.text: Jethro the Gentile priest of Midian comes to the camp, hears all the Lord has done, and confesses, 'Now I know the Lord is greater than all gods.' A pagan priest acknowledging the God of Israel — the firstfruits of the nations beginning to stream toward the mountain, even before the Law is given.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Religion of the Apostles

question: Jethro was an Egyptian priest.

memoryAid.text: Egypt is the house of bondage Moses fled FROM; Midian the land he fled TO. A priest of Egypt would serve the very gods God shattered in the plagues — so Jethro, who blesses the Lord, must belong to Midian, never to Pharaoh's house. Flee-from versus flee-to.

question: Of what land was Jethro a priest?

memoryAid.text: MIDIAN is where Moses 'hid in' after Pharaoh sought his life; its priest is Jethro. The sound-link hid-in / Midian holds the answer fast.

question: What were the names of Moses' two sons?

memoryAid.text: Two names, one confession of the exile's life: Gershom, 'a stranger there,' names the sojourning; Eliezer, 'God is my help,' names the deliverance. Together the sons preach Moses' whole story — a stranger in a strange land whom God rescued.

question: What does the name of Moses' son Gershom mean?

memoryAid.text: GerSHOM = 'a stranger SHOWing up there.' Moses named him saying, 'I have been a sojourner in a foreign land' — the name is the pilgrim's own confession.

question: What does the name of Moses' son Eliezer mean?

memoryAid.text: ELI-ezer: 'Eli' is my God, and 'ezer' is help — 'God is my help.' Split the name and the meaning falls out: a thanksgiving for rescue from Pharaoh's sword, turned into a child.

question: Jethro advised Moses to appoint rulers over groups of what four sizes?

memoryAid.text: Descend the ladder by tens: thousands, hundreds, fifties, tens — 1000, 100, 50, 10. The counting-down image fixes the four ranks in order, the people of God mustered into divisions for the journey ahead.

question: Why did Jethro advise Moses to appoint judges over the people?
answer: To share the burden of leadership
options: To share the burden of leadership
To raise an army for war
To collect taxes for the tabernacle
To choose a successor for Moses

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses as a single judgment-seat with a line of complainants stretching to the horizon, the meekest man on earth wearing thin under the whole multitude. Jethro's fix: SHARE the load before the shepherd is consumed — divide the burden among many heads so no one man is worn away.

question: In which month after leaving Egypt did Israel arrive at the wilderness of Sinai?

memoryAid.text: ‘Third month’ names the month of Sivan — not ‘three months later.’ Count fifty days from Passover (Lev 23:15–16) and you land in that very month, so Israel reaches Sinai right at the fifty-day mark: the Law is given about fifty days after the first Passover, written by the finger of God on tablets of stone. Blessed Augustine sees the pattern completed at Pentecost — fifty days after Christ’s Pascha the same ‘finger of God,’ the Holy Spirit, writes the law on hearts of flesh.
memoryAid.source: Blessed Augustine, Letter 55 (to Januarius); cf. Leviticus 23:15–16

question: God said He bore Israel out of Egypt on what?

memoryAid.text: The eagle does not carry its young in its talons below but stirs them onto its own back, putting itself between them and the hunter's arrow so every blow strikes the eagle first. Picture God carrying Israel where the danger hits Him, and the destination is not Canaan but Himself: 'I bore you on eagles' wings, and brought you unto Myself.'

question: According to Exodus 19:6, God said Israel would be to Him a royal priesthood and what kind of nation?
